entries.prompt: The Stoics believed that we must think before we speak. How do you discipline yourself to keep from speaking when what you have to say is more opinion than truth or is informed by emotion not reason?
entries.response:
Add pass/fail rules into the formulation of a statement or response. Can the emotion be separated from the opinion and stripped down to just what you believe to be true. Check what is the motivation behind what is about to be said. If motivated by a virtue then express it, if by the fantasy of an outcome then don't. Quick-wittedness should be internal to discern a response not to be a smartarse or derail to 'win' the discussion. Does the comment make good calculated sense, is it resourceful (not obvious). If it passes these then it is a wise contribution to the flow of discourse.


To gain wisdom, we must be willing to learn and adopt a beginner's mindset.

Give up bias, my way being the only way, hold my beer, scoffing at the way it's being done, that I think I know this, know it all, that my past experience is relevant, that the learned theory is applicable, that I will owe them somehow if they aren't told that I already know this, that not admitting that I learned something retains power, believing there is no such thing as power dynamic between two individuals. Stop pre-judging their judgement of you (that you'll lose their respect). Give up that it needs to be perfect, good enough may be faster or that it needs to be efficient when quality may be more important than time or cost.

entries.prompt: If we are open to learning, we can observe and learn from our own mistakes and those of others. What are some lessons that you learned from your own mistakes or the mistakes of those around you? How did these help redirect you?
entries.response:
My own mistakes
•  What needs to then be dropped to action correcting the mistake
•  The pivot to short term goal in the direction of long term goal
•  That cool heads prevailed and a can-do attitude
•  To search for opportunities that may only present in a time of crisis/recovery
•  That challenge is good opportunity to test tenacity, justice (taking ownership), wisdom (re-calculating) Others' mistakes
•  Present opportunities that would never be presented as you would not have made that mistake.
•  Chance to demonstrate that you are prepared to forgo your intentions to be their for them and follow their instructions, get hands dirty, do what it takes to overcome (attack the problem with strategy like minor birds) and come together as a shared building of character / camaraderie in moment.

entries.prompt: When it comes to knowledge, the Stoics had a 'garbage in, garbage out' perspective. How do you ensure that you are taking in the best quality information that emphasises truth.
entries.response: Get info from a variety of sources, media types, tribes. Focus on data/facts and not the interpretation of it or the projected circumstances. Note the reporters bias when learning their opinion. Note and check my own bias when taking in the info. Imagine being in their shoes even if I disagree with message. Check my mood before receiving message. Pause to see if I am just taking pleasure in being agreed with or pleasure in shared spitefulness/distain. Did I seek this info or was it fed to me from the algorithm feed.

entries.prompt: Sometimes we can get caught up in projecting the appearance of wisdom rather than focussing on true wisdom which doesn't need to be acknowledged.  What egoistic needs do you need to let go of in order to achieve true wisdom, not just it's appearance?
entries.response: Let go of the need to fit in. The need to shock or spark debate. The need to be contradictory. The need to impress or demonstrate that I don't need them to like me. Let go of the need to be intellectually funny. Let go of unnecessary terminology that will exclude who don't know. Let go of trying to win or steal their thunder. Let go of the need to preface the source of the knowledge or quote reference to prove credentials or that I'm well read. Let go of questions met I already know the answer to or a leading question for a practiced argument.

entries.prompt: When we begin to think we know it all, we stop learning. How do you keep your mind open to receiving new wisdom.

You can never cross the same river twice. ie Every new moment is a chance to use your experience as a starting point to be prepared but then do not drop your attention from the specifics on the day that someone may have some useful knowledge of. The external & internal influences of all parties involved in this activity today will/can effect the outcome in a positive or negative way and some of those negative you may not have encountered before and some of most positives may cancel them out. If you are the leader use delegation to bolster the team knowledge/trust/experience and free up attention to all levels. Progress will happen whether you do or not.

The Stoics believed that it was foolish not to blame others, but to blame oneself when things do not turn out the way you wished. use below to forgive yourself for something that you blamed yourself for in the past.
entries.response: Blamed myself for not reaching out. Blamed myself for being ignorant to others feelings & being selfish. Blamed myself for bringing dishonour to the family name & being lazy. Blamed myself for not seeing what was obvious and it bringing harm. Blamed myself for not sticking to it and following through on what I said. Blamed myself when in denial that it was a loved one who caused it. Blamed myself when it is easier to accept than the truth. I forgive myself for taking the less painful, less real opinion in the past. Forgive myself for when I would not accept truth. Blame is a way of explaining things that require more consideration and effort to properly determine root cause.

entries.prompt: Have you noticed the connection between your more disciplined mind and your mood? When you control your thoughts and feelings, how does it affect your outlook on life?
entries.response:
Don't feel the same shame. Outlook is more positive as I have found a fulfilment & patience. Outlook is can-do. Journalling has used up some inquisitive self examination in a meaningful way which has meant less often intrusive thoughts. Take things less personally. Moments of escalated anger have lessened but are more noticeable as they stand out.
Outlook is toward corrective action going forward not shame/disappointment for failing. Noticed that accumulated extenuating circumstances will still get me.

entries.prompt: The Stoics believed that our actions are more important than our words. When was a time you wished you had talked less and acted more?
entries.response: Telling people about diet/exercise plans. Sharing ideas for inventions. Explaining good examples to the kids instead of being a good example. Saying or re-living the pride I take on how I used to be flexible instead of applying that flexibility now. Saying I am going to start now on a long term goal out loud. Over-planning, overusing the preparation stage of a goal which drains the energy/motivation from taking the first step. Talking to myself about something to get the feel-good hit despite not having actually doing the action yet.

entries.prompt: What are you doing to expand your mind on a regular basis?
entries.response: Variety of news sources. If a curiosity presents, follow it. Use AI/Grok/Wikipedia to summarise. Be in the moment with full attention of all senses. Put people at ease to draw them out, letting them speak/share and having patience to do so. Be responsive where it is in service to the moment - not with any premeditation or opportunistic intentions. Resist immediately questioning the veracity - despite difference compared to own source of facts. Challenge myself to something now and to seek for the service to others/community by observing the moment.
